what_it_does: Describes the current state of an existing interface prototype (the fork) as background context for re-architecting: a document/comment review surface, realtime operator-lead chat, document switcher, and auto-reload, all hard-wired to a single channel and built as a monolith.
observations: It is a background note, not a plan.
Lists four interface capabilities: addressable/commentable board documents, realtime two-way chat over channel fabric, hamburger document switcher, live auto-reload on deploy.
States the interface is served on a tailnet and on Tim's phone.
Notes comments carry a typed envelope with target address, scale, tapped text, author.
Notes chat uses SSE pushes to browser, responding indicator, image attach.
Explicitly says it is hard-wired to one channel and is a fast-built monolith.
Frames the existing implementation as proof and material to be re-homed, not to be copied.
standouts: The 'do not copy, understand' framing of the fork.
Hard-wired single-channel constraint is identified as the key architectural mismatch.
Realtime chat over channel fabric with SSE and mobile push is already working.
conventions: Follows ledger item schema with YAML frontmatter (id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created/updated, history).
Uses markdown body with a top-level heading and prose paragraphs.
History records edits with from/to/by/ts/note fields.
Links use kind 'part_of' and board:// URI target.
concerns: Title changed from plan-oriented to background-only, but file path/id still suggest an item in a sequence; scope boundary is implicit.
No explicit list of problems or principles despite the edit note claiming 'background/problems/principles'.
Relies on proper nouns ('Tim's phone', 'tailnet') without defining them for readers outside the session.
notes: Background ledger entry capturing the current fork's interface capabilities and architectural debt before redesign.
questions: What is the exact architecture the current interface is not built under?
Which channel is it hard-wired to, and what does 'channel fabric' mean in this codebase?
What are the intended principles that should guide re-homing?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is background on what the interface currently is; actual content matches, though it also implies problems and principles without enumerating them.
apparent_themes: Separation of proof-of-concept from target design
intention_signals: It is trying to establish a shared, non-prescriptive baseline of the existing working interface so that redesign discussions can refer to proven behaviors without treating the prototype as the final architecture.
novelty: Explicitly reframes a working monolithic prototype as material to be re-homed rather than a reference implementation.
contribution: Provides a neutral inventory of already-validated user-facing behaviors (commentable documents, realtime chat, switcher, auto-reload) and an explicit warning about the single-channel monolith constraint, which a fused system can use as requirements and constraints rather than starting from scratch.
summary_for_embedding: A ledger background note describing an existing monolithic interface prototype with addressable document comments, realtime operator-lead chat, document switcher, and deploy auto-reload, all hard-wired to one channel and framed as proof-of-concept material for re-architecting.
intention_for_embedding: To capture the current fork's interface capabilities and architectural debt as a shared baseline before redesign.
suggested_fields: A 'scope' or 'document_kind' field to distinguish background notes from plans/decisions.
A 'principles' or 'constraints' field to capture the implied architectural rules and problems explicitly.
A 'related_systems' or 'prototype_location' field to record where the fork/interface is served.
proposed_edge_kinds: documents (the file documents a system or prototype)
constrains (the background imposes constraints on a redesign target)
